title: Security-Focused Patch Management Process
content:
# Security-Focused Patch Management Process

## Overview

Patch management is a foundational security control that reduces the attack surface by addressing known vulnerabilities in operating systems, applications, and firmware. Unpatched vulnerabilities are consistently among the top initial access vectors for attackers. According to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, actively exploited vulnerabilities demand immediate attention.

This guide covers patch management from a security operations perspective, including prioritization, SLA definitions, emergency patching procedures, and integration with vulnerability management and SOC operations.

## Patch Prioritization Framework

### Risk-Based Prioritization Matrix

| CVSS Score | Exploitation Status | Asset Criticality | Patch SLA |
|------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------|
| 9.0-10.0 (Critical) | Actively exploited (KEV) | Any | 24-48 hours |
| 9.0-10.0 (Critical) | POC available | Internet-facing | 72 hours |
| 9.0-10.0 (Critical) | POC available | Internal | 7 days |
| 9.0-10.0 (Critical) | No known exploit | Any | 14 days |
| 7.0-8.9 (High) | Actively exploited (KEV) | Any | 72 hours |
| 7.0-8.9 (High) | POC available | Internet-facing | 7 days |
| 7.0-8.9 (High) | POC available | Internal | 14 days |
| 7.0-8.9 (High) | No known exploit | Any | 30 days |
| 4.0-6.9 (Medium) | Actively exploited | Any | 7 days |
| 4.0-6.9 (Medium) | No known exploit | Any | 60 days |
| 0.1-3.9 (Low) | Any | Any | 90 days |

### CISA KEV Integration

CISA maintains the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, which lists vulnerabilities with confirmed active exploitation. Federal agencies must remediate KEV entries within specified timelines. All organizations should treat KEV entries as highest priority.

```bash
# Download current KEV catalog
curl -o kev_catalog.json https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json

# Parse for vulnerabilities due within 7 days
python3 -c "
import json, datetime
with open('kev_catalog.json') as f:
    kev = json.load(f)
today = datetime.date.today()
for vuln in kev['vulnerabilities']:
    due = datetime.date.fromisoformat(vuln['dueDate'])
    if (due - today).days <= 7 and (due - today).days >= 0:
        print(f\"{vuln['cveID']} | {vuln['vendorProject']} | {vuln['product']} | Due: {vuln['dueDate']}\")
"
```

### Exploit Prediction Scoring System (EPSS)

EPSS provides a probability score (0-1) indicating the likelihood a vulnerability will be exploited in the next 30 days. Combine EPSS with CVSS for better prioritization:

| CVSS | EPSS | Priority |
|------|------|----------|
| High + | > 0.5 | Immediate -- very likely to be exploited |
| High + | 0.1 - 0.5 | Urgent -- moderate exploitation likelihood |
| High + | < 0.1 | Standard -- low exploitation likelihood despite severity |
| Medium | > 0.5 | Urgent -- high exploitation likelihood |
| Medium | < 0.5 | Standard priority |
| Low | Any | Scheduled maintenance window |

## Patch Management Workflow

### Standard Patch Cycle

```
Day 1: Patch Tuesday (or vendor release)
  |
  +-- Vendor releases security patches
  |
  +-- Security team reviews advisories
  |   - Identify affected systems from asset inventory
  |   - Assess CVSS scores and exploitation status
  |   - Cross-reference with CISA KEV
  |   - Check EPSS scores
  |
Day 1-2: Risk Assessment and Prioritization
  |
  +-- Categorize patches by priority tier
  +-- Identify systems requiring immediate patching
  +-- Identify patches requiring testing before deployment
  +-- Create patch deployment plan
  |
Day 2-5: Testing
  |
  +-- Deploy to test environment
  +-- Validate application compatibility
  +-- Verify patch does not break critical functions
  +-- Document test results
  |
Day 5-7: Pilot Deployment
  |
  +-- Deploy to pilot group (5-10% of fleet)
  +-- Monitor for issues for 24-48 hours
  +-- Collect feedback from pilot users
  |
Day 7-14: Production Deployment
  |
  +-- Deploy to production in waves
  +-- Monitor deployment success rate
  +-- Track exceptions and failures
  |
Day 14+: Verification and Compliance
  |
  +-- Verify patch installation on all target systems
  +-- Rescan for vulnerabilities
  +-- Document exceptions with risk acceptance
  +-- Report compliance metrics
```

### Emergency Patch Procedure

For actively exploited critical vulnerabilities:

```
Zero-day or KEV announcement
  |
  +-- HOUR 0-2: Assessment
  |   - Identify all affected systems
  |   - Determine exposure (internet-facing, internal, air-gapped)
  |   - Assess available mitigations if patch not yet available
  |
  +-- HOUR 2-4: Mitigation
  |   - If patch available: begin emergency testing
  |   - If patch NOT available: implement workarounds
  |     (WAF rules, network segmentation, feature disable,
  |      detection rules, increased monitoring)
  |
  +-- HOUR 4-12: Emergency Testing
  |   - Abbreviated test cycle on representative systems
  |   - Focus on critical application compatibility
  |   - Document known issues
  |
  +-- HOUR 12-24: Rapid Deployment
  |   - Deploy to internet-facing systems first
  |   - Deploy to critical internal systems
  |   - Deploy to remaining systems
  |
  +-- HOUR 24-48: Verification
  |   - Confirm patch installation
  |   - Verify vulnerability is remediated
  |   - Remove temporary mitigations
  |   - Monitor for exploitation attempts
  |
  +-- Post-Emergency Review
      - Document timeline and decisions
      - Identify process improvements
      - Update emergency patch procedures
```

## Patching by Technology

### Operating Systems

**Windows (WSUS/SCCM/Intune)**:
```powershell
# Check pending Windows updates
Get-WindowsUpdate -MicrosoftUpdate

# Install specific KB
Install-WindowsUpdate -KBArticleID "KB5034441" -AcceptAll -AutoReboot

# Check installed updates
Get-HotFix | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending | Select-Object -First 20

# Verify specific patch is installed
Get-HotFix -Id "KB5034441"

# Check Windows Update history
Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_QuickFixEngineering | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending
```

**Linux (RHEL/Ubuntu)**:
```bash
# RHEL/CentOS: List available security updates
sudo dnf updateinfo list security

# RHEL/CentOS: Install only security patches
sudo dnf update --security

# RHEL/CentOS: Install specific advisory
sudo dnf update --advisory RHSA-2024:XXXX

# Ubuntu: List available security updates
sudo apt list --upgradable 2>/dev/null | grep -i security

# Ubuntu: Install security updates only
sudo apt-get upgrade -s 2>/dev/null | grep -i security
sudo unattended-upgrades --dry-run

# Check installed patch level
uname -r
rpm -qa --last | head -20  # RHEL
apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | head -20  # Ubuntu

# Check for kernel live patches (no reboot required)
sudo kpatch list  # RHEL
sudo canonical-livepatch status  # Ubuntu
```

**macOS**:
```bash
# List available updates
softwareupdate -l

# Install all available updates
sudo softwareupdate -ia

# Install specific update
sudo softwareupdate -i "macOS Sequoia 15.3-15.3"

# Check OS version
sw_vers
```

### Third-Party Applications

| Application Category | Patch Source | Automation |
|---------------------|-------------|------------|
| Web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge) | Auto-update built-in | GPO/MDM to enforce auto-update |
| Java Runtime | Oracle/Adoptium releases | SCCM/Intune deployment |
| Adobe products | Adobe Admin Console | SCCM/Intune deployment |
| Office suites | Microsoft Update / Click-to-Run | Office Update channel GPO |
| PDF readers | Vendor update mechanism | SCCM/Intune deployment |
| VPN clients | Vendor releases | SCCM/Intune deployment |

### Network and Infrastructure

| Device Type | Patch Method | Considerations |
|------------|-------------|----------------|
| Firewalls | Vendor management console | Maintenance window, HA failover, config backup |
| Switches/Routers | CLI or management platform | Rolling upgrade for HA pairs, TFTP firmware upload |
| Load Balancers | Vendor management console | Active-passive failover during upgrade |
| WAF | Vendor management console | Signature updates may be separate from firmware |
| IoT/OT devices | Vendor-specific | May require extended testing, may not be patchable |

## Vulnerability-Patch Lifecycle Integration

### SOC and Vulnerability Management Handoff

| Phase | Vulnerability Management | SOC Operations |
|-------|------------------------|----------------|
| Discovery | Vulnerability scan identifies CVE | -- |
| Prioritization | Assign priority based on CVSS/EPSS/KEV | Add detection rules for exploitation attempts |
| Remediation | Deploy patch or workaround | Monitor for exploitation during remediation window |
| Verification | Rescan confirms remediation | Validate detection rules trigger correctly |
| Exception | Risk acceptance for unpatchable systems | Enhanced monitoring for excepted systems |

### Detection During Patching Window

While vulnerabilities are being patched, SOC should implement compensating detection:

```
# Example: Detection rule for CVE exploitation while patch is pending

# 1. Check if exploit signatures are available
#    - Update IDS/IPS rules from vendor
#    - Deploy Snort/Suricata rules if available

# 2. Create SIEM correlation rule
alert_rule:
  name: "Exploitation attempt for unpatched CVE-YYYY-XXXXX"
  condition:
    source: IDS_alerts OR proxy_logs OR endpoint_telemetry
    pattern: [CVE-specific IOCs or traffic patterns]
    target_system: [list of unpatched systems]
  action: P1 alert to SOC

# 3. Increase monitoring on vulnerable systems
#    - Enable verbose logging
#    - Reduce alert thresholds
#    - Add to watchlist for manual review
```

## Patch Compliance Metrics

| Metric | Target | Measurement | Reporting |
|--------|--------|-------------|----------|
| Critical patch compliance (14 days) | >95% | Patched within SLA / total affected | Weekly |
| High patch compliance (30 days) | >90% | Patched within SLA / total affected | Weekly |
| Medium patch compliance (60 days) | >85% | Patched within SLA / total affected | Monthly |
| Mean time to patch (critical) | <7 days | Average days from release to deployment | Weekly |
| Mean time to patch (high) | <14 days | Average days from release to deployment | Monthly |
| Patch failure rate | <5% | Failed deployments / total deployments | Per cycle |
| Exception rate | <3% | Systems with risk acceptance / total systems | Monthly |
| KEV compliance | 100% | KEV entries remediated within CISA timeline | Weekly |
| Reboot pending rate | <10% | Systems pending reboot / total patched | Weekly |

## Common Patching Challenges

1. **Reboot-averse systems** -- Critical systems cannot be rebooted during business hours. Implement kernel live patching where available, schedule maintenance windows, and use HA configurations to enable rolling updates.

2. **Legacy systems without patches** -- End-of-life systems with no vendor support. Implement compensating controls: network segmentation, enhanced monitoring, application allowlisting, virtual patching via WAF/IPS.

3. **Patch breaks application** -- Compatibility issue discovered after deployment. Maintain rollback procedures, test with application owners before production, maintain a known-issue database.

4. **Shadow IT and unmanaged devices** -- Systems not in asset inventory not receiving patches. Deploy network access control (NAC), run discovery scans, integrate asset management with patch management.

5. **Third-party application patching** -- No centralized management for non-OS patches. Deploy third-party patch management tools, maintain application inventory, automate where possible.

6. **Distributed/remote workforce** -- Endpoints not on corporate network miss patch deployments. Use cloud-based patch management (Intune, Workspace ONE), VPN-less patching, peer-to-peer distribution.

## Compliance Framework Requirements

| Framework | Patch Requirement | Reference |
|-----------|------------------|----------|
| PCI-DSS v4.0 | Install critical patches within one month of release | Requirement 6.3.3 |
| HIPAA | Implement procedures for guarding against malicious software | 164.308(a)(5)(ii)(B) |
| NIST 800-171 | Identify, report, and correct system flaws in a timely manner | 3.14.1 |
| ISO 27001 | Management of technical vulnerabilities | A.12.6.1 |
| SOC 2 | System components are monitored and maintained | CC7.1 |
| CMMC | Perform periodic and timely remediation of vulnerabilities | 3.14.1 |
| CIS Controls | Perform automated operating system patch management | Control 7.3 |

## References

- CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog: https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog
- FIRST EPSS: https://www.first.org/epss/
- NIST SP 800-40 Rev 4: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Planning
- CIS Controls v8: Control 7 (Continuous Vulnerability Management)
- Microsoft Security Update Guide: https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/
- Red Hat Security Advisories: https://access.redhat.com/security/security-updates/
- Ubuntu Security Notices: https://ubuntu.com/security/notices
